era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the Hundred Years' War in its final phase, with English forces still dominant but facing increasing French resistance. The Hussite Wars continue in Bohemia following the execution of Jan Hus in 1415. The Italian Renaissance is in its early stages. Ming China under the Xuande Emperor is consolidating administrative control. The Ottoman Empire is expanding in the Balkans. This is a period of religious reformation, military conflict, and cultural transition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
Southeast Asia (limited chronicle material in Western sources)
Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse documentation)

events.title: English siege of Orléans begins
events.region: Orléans, Loire Valley, Kingdom of France
events.description: In 1428, English forces under the command of William de la Pole and John Talbot began a siege of the city of Orléans, a key stronghold in the Loire Valley. The siege would last until 1429 and become one of the most famous sieges of the Hundred Years' War. Orléans was strategically important because it controlled access to the Loire Valley and was a major French stronghold. The English siege was part of a broader strategy to consolidate English control over France. The siege would be broken by French forces under Joan of Arc in 1429, marking a turning point in the war.
key_figures: Jean de Dunois (French commander, 'the Bastard of Orléans')

sources.name: English government records and military reports

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the English siege of Orléans in 1428 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. The date and circumstances are well-documented.
cross_references: 1429-001 (Relief of Orléans by Joan of Arc)

events.title: Hussite Wars; Battle of Tachov
events.description: In 1428, Hussite forces engaged in military campaigns against Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. The Battle of Tachov (also known as the Battle of Tachau) in 1428 was one of several engagements in the ongoing Hussite Wars. The Hussite forces, under commanders like Prokop the Great, continued to demonstrate military effectiveness through their innovative tactics, including the use of wagon-fortresses and early firearms. The conflict remained unresolved, with neither side achieving a decisive victory.
key_figures: Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic response)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Tachov in 1428 is attested in Hussite sources, though the exact date and details may vary. The broader context of the Hussite Wars is confirmed.

events.title: Xuande Emperor's reign in Ming China; administrative consolidation
events.description: The Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435) was consolidating Ming administrative control in 1428. His reign is characterized by efforts to strengthen central authority, improve bureaucratic efficiency, and maintain the stability achieved under his predecessor, the Yongle Emperor. The Xuande period saw the continuation of Zheng He's treasure voyages, though the emperor was less enthusiastic about maritime expansion than his predecessor. The emperor was also an accomplished artist and patron of the arts, contributing to the cultural flourishing of the Ming Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: The Xuande Emperor's reign and his administrative policies are well-documented in Ming records. The general context of his rule is confirmed.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans; consolidation of power
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad II (r. 1421–1451) was consolidating its control over the Balkans in 1428. The Ottomans were expanding their territorial holdings and eliminating remaining Byzantine and Venetian strongholds. This period saw the Ottoman military machine becoming increasingly effective, with the development of the Janissary corps and the use of gunpowder weapons. The Ottoman expansion was part of a broader process that would culminate in the fall of Constantinople in 1453.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans in 1428 is attested in Ottoman and Venetian sources, though specific events in 1428 are less precisely documented than in other years.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration; continued African coast exploration
events.description: Prince Henry of Portugal continued to sponsor maritime expeditions along the African coast in 1428. Portuguese explorers were systematically mapping the West African coast and establishing trading relationships with local rulers. The expeditions were motivated by commercial interests (gold, spices) and religious zeal (crusade against Islam). By 1428, Portuguese ships had reached the coast of what is now Senegal and Guinea. These early expeditions were laying the groundwork for the Portuguese slave trade and the Age of Discovery.
key_figures: Henry the Navigator (Prince of Portugal, patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (mid-15th century, but based on contemporary records)
Portuguese royal records and navigation logs

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast in 1428 is attested in later chronicles and cartographic evidence. Specific expeditions in 1428 are less precisely dated than the broader context of exploration.

disconfirming_evidence: The English siege of Orléans, while significant, is sometimes portrayed as a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, but this overstates its immediate impact. The siege lasted until 1429 and was broken by French forces, but the war continued for another 24 years. The characterization of the Hussite Wars as a conflict between 'reformers' and 'conservatives' obscures the complex factional divisions within the Hussite movement. The Battle of Tachov in 1428 is attested in Hussite sources, but the exact date and significance are debated. Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is often presented as inevitable, but this obscures the resistance of local populations and the contingency of Ottoman military successes. Portuguese exploration is often presented as a precursor to the Age of Discovery, but this teleological framing obscures the fact that Portuguese expansion was driven primarily by commercial and religious motives.
historiographic_note: The year 1428 is moderately documented for Western Europe (the English siege of Orléans is well-recorded) and moderately documented for East Asia (Ming records are comprehensive). Ottoman sources are less detailed for this period, and Venetian records are fragmentary. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. English and French sources on the siege of Orléans reflect the biases of their origins. Ming records are official court documents that emphasize imperial achievements. Ottoman sources are often compiled retrospectively. Portuguese exploration records are fragmentary and often compiled retrospectively. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this year.

graph_edges.note: The English siege of Orléans in 1428 was broken by Joan of Arc's relief in 1429
The Battle of Tachov in 1428 was part of the ongoing Hussite Wars that continued through 1430
The Xuande Emperor's administrative consolidation was concurrent with Zheng He's voyages
Ottoman expansion in the Balkans in 1428 was part of the process leading to the fall of Constantinople in 1453
